column1: # raw: Backlash proxy data for intermediate historical shocks (1917–1992)
# source: NAACP annual reports (lynching data); Klarman (2004) From Jim Crow to Civil Rights
#         (White Citizens' Council formation); Gallup/Pew crime panic polling;
#   shock_year: The primary political shock event year
#   backlash_index: Raw backlash magnitude in native units (documented per shock below)
#   source_note: Primary source for this observation
#   1917 (Red Summer): NAACP-documented racial violence incidents per year (Moore 2003 estimate)
#   1954 (Brown v. Board): White Citizens' Council chapters formed per year (Klarman 2004)
#   1968 (MLK assassination): % Gallup "law and order" as most important problem (Gallup)
#   1992 (Rodney King): Federal "tough on crime" bill count per Congress (DOJ legislative tracker)
# retrieval: Author compilation from published academic estimates and historical records

column4: source_note
NAACP 1919 Thirty Years of Lynching report — racial violence incidents documented
NAACP annual report — wartime racial violence surge East St Louis aftermath
Red Summer peak — NAACP documented 83 lynchings + urban race riots
Post-Red Summer — KKK revival beginning (Trelease 1971 estimate)
KKK national membership reaching 100k; Tulsa Race Massacre
KKK membership surge to 3M (Trelease 1971); anti-Black legislation wave
KKK at peak political influence — multiple gubernatorial wins
KKK convention; national immigration restriction (Johnson-Reed Act)
KKK decline begins after Stephenson scandal
KKK membership declining; backlash institutionalized into Jim Crow codes
Backlash absorbed into legal architecture (separate-but-equal solidified)
Low-level maintenance backlash
Trough — backlash encoded in law; active violence subsides
Brown decision — immediate legislative response minimal (shock year)
Southern Manifesto preparation; White Citizens' Council chapter formations
Southern Manifesto signed by 101 legislators; WCC membership ~250k (Klarman 2004)
Little Rock crisis; Eisenhower federalizes National Guard — peak legal resistance
WCC at organizational peak; massive resistance legislation across 11 states
Last WCC membership peak; private segregation academies opening
Sit-in movement triggers escalating white violence; WCC membership declining
Freedom Rides attacked; legal massive resistance failing
Ole Miss crisis; federal enforcement beginning to overcome state resistance
Birmingham bombings; backlash intensifying but losing institutional ground
Civil Rights Act passage — massive-resistance era formally ends
Voting Rights Act — legal backlash era completed; shift to coded resistance
Nixon law-and-order campaign; Gallup 29% name crime/law as top issue
Nixon Southern Strategy active; crime panic at national peak
No-knock laws; Gallup crime fear index peak; anti-busing legislation
Nixon war on drugs declared; mandatory minimum proposals
Nixon landslide partly on law-and-order; busing as code issue
Watergate distraction reduces crime-panic legislation temporarily
Ford continues law-and-order emphasis; crack cocaine legislation drafted
Organized crime panic shifts focus
Carter election; crime panic subsiding slightly
Carter criminal justice reform attempts
Proposition 13 shifts backlash to tax revolt frame
Trough — crime panic absorbed into mandatory minimum sentencing frameworks
Rodney King verdict; LA uprising — immediate legislative response beginning
Clinton crime bill drafted; Three-Strikes proposals multiplying
Violent Crime Control Act signed — 100k police; truth-in-sentencing incentives
Three-strikes laws in 24 states; prison population growth accelerating
Welfare Reform Act (TANF) — secondary legislative backlash wave
Prison privatization expansion; backlash institutionalized
Crime rates declining but incarceration continues rising
Backlash absorbed into mass incarceration as structural equilibrium
Trough — crime panic mechanisms encoded in mandatory minimums and three-strikes
